nameMeanings.name: Cord
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): nickname or metonymic occupational name from Middle English (Old French) corde ‘rope, cord, string’, possibly given to someone who wore a cord (round the waist) or who made ropes, bowstrings, etc. See Corder .
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Mac Cuairt or Mac Cuarta, see McCord .

variants.name: Coard
meanings: In some cases possibly also French: variant of Couard, an unflattering nickname from Old French couard ‘coward, poltroon’, a compound of coe ‘tail’ + the pejorative suffix -ard.

variants.name: Cordell
meanings: English: occupational name for a maker of cord or string or a nickname for an habitual wearer of decorative ties and ribbons, from Old French cordele, a diminutive of corde ‘cord’ (see Cord ).
Americanized form of German   Kardel   and   Kardell  .

variants.name: Cordes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Eldor, Erwin, Heinz, Kurt, Deitrich, Dieter, Ewald, Franz, Fritz, Gerhard, Gunther, Heinrich.
English (Middlesex and Surrey): variant of Cord , with post-medieval excrescent -s.
German: variant of   Kordes   and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this. This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine).

variants.name: Corder
meanings: English (Essex): occupational name from Middle English corder ‘rope maker’ (Old French cordier).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: William Cord was born in 1636, in London, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 daughter with Ann. He died in 1675, in Maryland, British Colonial America, at the age of 39.
